coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical driven simple preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/SnO2 nanocomposite: A novel electrocatalyst for sensitive voltammetric determination of riboflavin
coredata.dc:creator: Muthusankar, Ganesan
Rajkumar, Chellakannu
Chen, Shen-Ming
Karkuzhali, Rajendran
Gopu, Gopalakrishnan
Sangili, Arumugam
Sengottuvelan, Nallathambi
Sankar, Raman
coredata.dc:description: We report a simple preparation of novel nanocomposite for the sensitive determination of riboflavin (RF) in real samples. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/SnO2 (N-CQD/SnO2) nanocomposite was prepared using sonochemical approach and applied as a voltammetric sensor for the sensitive determination of RF for the first time. The crystallographic phase, functional groups, surface analysis, and elemental distribution were examined using XRD, FT-IR, HR-TEM and EDS spectroscopic techniques respectively. This N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite-modified electrode shows a fast and sensitive electrochemical response to RF sensing with a good sensitivity (2.496 μA μM−1 cm-2), wide linear range (0.05–306 μM), low detection limit (8 nM) and excellent anti-interference ability. Furthermore, the developed sensor was investigated in commercial riboflavin tablets and milk powder and obtained results are quite satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sonochemical preparation
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
SnO2
Riboflavin
Voltammetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EIS and CV studies on the modified electrodes
Proposed mechanism for electrooxidation of RF
Effect of film thickness and pH
Sensitivity and detection limit of RF sensor
Accumulation time, operational stability and reproducibility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the sonochemical preparation of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite and its electrochemical determination of RF in milk powder and tablets.
Electrooxidation mechanism of RF on N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) FT-IR spectrum of N-CQD, SnO2 NPs and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite.
HR-TEM image of (a) N-CQD, (b–d) N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite and (e) EDS spectrum of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite.
(a) Nyquist plots and (b) CVs of the different modified electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution.

(a) CVs of different films (b) CVs of different scan rates (10–200 mV s−1) in the presence of 25 μM RF in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at scan rate 50 mV s−1 (c) corresponding linear plot between the peak current and scan rate (mV s−1) and (d) log-log plots of the peak current vs. scan rate.

CVs of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode in PBS containing 25 μM RF (a) at a different film thickness of the nanocomposite (b) at various pH (3.0–11.0) and (c) calibration plot for pH vs. Epa and Ipa.
(a) DPVs of increasing RF concentration (a–o = 0.05–306 μM) and (b) calibration curve of the peak currents on the RF concentrations.

DPV response of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE for RF (a) at 2000 μM concentration of common interfering molecules and ions (b) their corresponding current responses and (c) increasing RF concentration (a – d = 50 – 200 μM) in the presence of 2000 μM concentration of AA, DA, and UA at higher working potential.

CVs obtained at N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 250 μM RF at (a) various accumulation time (B) five different electrodes (C) 50 nonstop cycles of without RF and (D) 50 nonstop cycles of with RF.
Comparison of analytical parameters between different modified electrodes for the determination of RF.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Method: DPV = differential pulse voltammetry, SWAdSV = square-wave adsorptive stripping voltammetry.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Modified electrode: p-AMTa electrode = 3-amino-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole modified glassy carbon electrode, PEDOT/ZrO2NPs/GCE = poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)/zirconia nanoparticles modified glassy carbon electrode, BiFE = bismuth film electrode, GC/MWCNTs-MnIIIsalen = glassy carbon/multiwalled carbon nanotubes-N, N' bis(salicylidene)ethylenediamine, MnO2/CPE = manganese oxide/carbon paste electrode, GC/MB-SO3H-MSM = glassy carbon electrode modified with methylene blue incorporated sulfonic acid functionalized mesoporous silica material, N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE = N-doped carbon quantum dot/tin oxide/screen printed carbon electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of RF content in pharmaceutical and nutrition products.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical driven simple preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/SnO2 nanocomposite: A novel electrocatalyst for sensitive voltammetric determination of riboflavin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, 630003, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Academia Sinica, Taipei, 11529, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a simple preparation of novel nanocomposite for the sensitive determination of riboflavin (RF) in real samples. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/SnO2 (N-CQD/SnO2) nanocomposite was prepared using sonochemical approach and applied as a voltammetric sensor for the sensitive determination of RF for the first time. The crystallographic phase, functional groups, surface analysis, and elemental distribution were examined using XRD, FT-IR, HR-TEM and EDS spectroscopic techniques respectively. This N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite-modified electrode shows a fast and sensitive electrochemical response to RF sensing with a good sensitivity (2.496 μA μM−1 cm-2), wide linear range (0.05–306 μM), low detection limit (8 nM) and excellent anti-interference ability. Furthermore, the developed sensor was investigated in commercial riboflavin tablets and milk powder and obtained results are quite satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, semiconductor-carbon heterostructure materials are gaining great research curiosity due to their charming properties and effective applicability in various research domains like solar cells, supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries and sensors [1]. When carbon materials combine with semiconducting transitional metal oxides (MnO2, RuO2, V2O5, TiO2, SnO2, and NiO), novel properties can be achieved because of the new heterostructure, which is highly favorable for practical applications. Among these metal oxides, tin oxide (SnO2) is an intensively studied n-type semiconducting material with a large band gap of 3.6 eV at 300 K. SnO2 is a low cost, nontoxic and high-capacitance material. Additionally, it has more positive conduction band edge, which makes it a better electron acceptor than TiO2 and ZnO [2]. These features make the SnO2 as a host material for dye-sensitized solar cells, gas sensors, transistors, transparent conducting electrodes, catalysts, supercapacitors, and so on [3]. Furthermore, SnO2 possess a low chemical potential, which makes it an efficient candidate in a fast redox reaction and good electrode material for an electrocatalytic application. In particular, SnO2 modified electrodes provides larger electrochemical surface area thus leading to higher sensitivity for target molecules. A further hybridization of SnO2 with carbon-nanomaterials such as graphene [4], functionalized graphene [5], graphene oxide [6], carbon nanotubes [7] can act as a mechanical buffer and also enhance its electrochemical performance in sensing applications.

In this concern, carbon quantum dots (CQD) are hopeful new carbon nanostructures showing exciting properties than their bulk carbon counterpart in photophysical properties like aqueous solubility, strong photoluminescence, resistance to photobleaching and higher electrical conductivity. In addition to that CQD possess chemical stability, biocompatibility, and less toxicity. Hence it can act as a potential candidate for numerous application in sensors, bio-sensors, catalysis, bio-imaging, electro and photocatalysis, optoelectronics and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [8–12]. Recent studies have shown that the electrodes modified with CQD can exhibit higher electrochemical sensing performance than bare GCE due to its unique electrical properties [13–15]. Moreover, it has been proved that the electrocatalytic performance of the CQD can be improved by nitrogen (N) doping. Recent studies also reveal the N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) and its composites can exhibit outstanding electrocatalytic activity [16,17]. Thus, it is expected that the introduction of N-CQD on SnO2 nanostructure can exhibit the improved electrocatalytic performance.

Riboflavin (RF) is the water-soluble vitamin (VB2), which helps for the conversion of fats, proteins, and carbohydrates into energy in human body. Even, RF itself converts into coenzymes (flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD)), which are important for the tissue respiration. The deficiency of the RF may chance to form the eye irritation, itching, sore tongue and skin damage [18]. Thus, the sensitive determination of RF level is highly needed to treat the diseases. Many analytical techniques have been developed for the determination of RF, including chemiluminescence [19], liquid chromatography [20], voltammetry [21], and fluorescence [22] method. Among these analytical techniques, electrochemical methods are an effective for the determination of RF, because of their sensitivity, selectivity, ease of use and reproducibility.

In this work, we report the preparation of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite by a simple ultrasonic assisted preparation method for the first time. Then the nanocomposite is fabricated on the SPCE to form an electrochemical sensor. The N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite-modified electrode is applied for the electrochemical determination of RF. It exhibits an enhanced electrocatalytic effect towards the oxidation of RF with an improved voltammetric response. The further key feature of the sensor is ease of preparation, wide linear range, and the low detection limit for the sensitive determination of RF.

The analytical grade reagents of tin chloride l-glutamic acid (C5H9NO4), ethylenediamine (C2H4(NH2)2) (EDA), (SnCl2. H2O) and riboflavin (C17H20N4O6) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as without further purification. A stock solution of RF (0.1 M) was prepared with double distilled water. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH were prepared by NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 and the pH values adjusted with 0.1 M NaOH. All the solutions were prepared using double distilled water.

Powder X-ray diffraction (P-XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded (XPERT – PRO; PANalytical B.V., Netherlands diffractometer) in Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the range between 20° to 80°. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker IFS28) was used to identify the functionalities present in the samples. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100 F, USA) was used for the surface analysis and the corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) also examined for the elemental analysis. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed using CHI 400 and 900 electrochemical workstation (CHI, USA). A three-electrode system comprising an SPCE coated with N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as a counter electrode were used. Electrochemical measurements were performed in PBS (pH 7.0) with inert gas (N2) condition.

The preparation method has adopted from our early report in literature with some modifications [23]. Briefly, 2 g of l-glutamic acid and 2 mL of EDA were dissolved in 40 mL of double distilled water and then stirred for 15 min., to get the homogenous mixture. Later, the mixture solution was transferred into an autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 6 h. A pale yellow colored solution is resulted, which is further purified by syringe filter having 0.22 μm pore size to get the pure N-CQD solution.

The preparation procedure was followed from the previously reported method with some modification [4]. Briefly, 1.5 M solution of SnCl2. 2H2O was prepared in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was stirred to get the homogeneous solution. To this solution, 15 mM of CTAB was added and pH of the solution was adjusted to pH 8, using 0.9 M NaOH solution. Further, the solution was stirred well to get the Sn(OH)2 precipitate, which is separated by centrifugation, washed with double distilled water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C. The dry product was mortared well and then calcinated at 600 °C for 4 h in order to obtain the pure SnO2 NPs.

0.3 g of as-prepared SnO2 NPs were dispersed in 15 mL of as-synthesized N-CQD solution and kept in ultrasonication for 1 h. The mixture was filtered and washed with deionized water thrice and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h to get the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite and its schematic representation is shown in Scheme 1 .

5 mg of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite was dispersed in 1 mL of double distilled water and treated in ultrasonication for 15 min. An optimized concentration of 5 μL of the suspension was drop cast on the screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) surface and then allowed to dry to obtain the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite modified SPCE (N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE).

Commercially available riboflavin and B complex tablets were purchased from the local drug store and milk powder (Nestle Foods, Taiwan) was purchased from the local supermarket. which were taken for the RF real sample analysis. The tablets were weighed, and then made into a fine powder with a mortar. The powder was mixed with 100 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 20 min. and then filtered using Whatman 1 (55 mm diameter) filter paper to get the clear solution. Further, the solution was diluted twice using PBS (pH 7) and the DPV was carried out using the standard addition method. A weighed amount of milk powder was dissolved in boiling water and the proteins were precipitated out by the addition of glacial acetic acid and digested for 15 min in a water bath. The solution was filtered using Whatman 1 (55 mm diameter) filter paper, pH of the diluted filtrate was adjusted with boric and orthophosphoric acid to pH 7 and then DPV studies were performed.

XRD measurement was carried out to understand the phase and structure of pristine SnO2 NPs, N-CQD and N-CQD decorated SnO2 nanocomposite. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD pattern of the SnO2 and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite. The diffraction peak of N-CQD displays a broad peak centered at 24° illustrates the amorphous nature of the system. However, the interplanar spacing of the (002) plan resembles the interlayer spacing of the graphite [24]. The observed diffraction peaks (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (002), (310), (112), (202), (320), and (321) in pristine SnO2 and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite are well matched to the standard data of JCPDs #00-041-1445, space group of P42/mnm, with the unit cell parameters a, b = 4.738 Å, and c = 3.187 Å with tetragonal rutile structure. Moreover, the broad diffraction peak of N-CQD can be indexed at 20° in the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite is a good agreement for the successful incorporation of the N-CQD on the surface of the SnO2 NPs. The peak shift of N-CQD from 24° to 20° also indicates the interaction might be happened between the N-CQD and SnO2 NPs to result in the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite. Further, there is no trace of impurity peak is observed, which suggests the good crystallinity of the samples. The average crystallite size was calculated using the Scherrer formula
D = 0.9λ/β cos θ
Where λ ( = 1.5406) is the Cu Kα radiation wavelength, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in a radian of the peak with (hkl) value, θ is the diffraction angle. The average crystallite size of the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite was found to be 23.5 nm.

The functionalities of the N-CQD, SnO2, and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite were analyzed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b). The FT-IR peaks around 3300 cm−1 and 1630 cm-1 are found in N-CQD, SnO2 and N-CQD/SnO2nanocomposite corresponds to the stretching and deformation mode of -O–H functional group respectively. A small hump around 1100 cm−1 both in SnO2 and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite are due to the presence of oxygen over the surface (Sn–Osurf). The metal oxide peaks for SnO2 resulted at 633 and 521 cm−1 due to the asymmetric stretching of Sn–O–Sn and symmetric stretching of Sn–O respectively [25]. Whereas, N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite showed the Sn–O–Sn asymmetric stretching at a slightly higher frequency at 652 cm−1 suggesting the surface modification. The N-CQD shows the peaks at 3290, 1576, 1408 and 1308 cm-1 which are attributed to the -N–H, -CO, -CC and CN stretching vibration respectively [26]. Whereas, in the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite, the functional group's vibration of N-CQD can be seen with the exception of -C = O group (Fig. 1b), indicating the interaction of N-CQD with SnO2 has happened through the carbonyl functional group. This may be the reason for the shifting of SnOSn asymmetric stretching towards the higher frequency.

The morphological features of N-CQD, SnO2 NPs, and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite were analyzed using HR-TEM. Fig. 2 a shows the HR-TEM images of N-CQD and Fig. 2b-d presents the HR-TEM images of N-CQD/SnO2 at different magnification. The N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite exhibits spherical morphology with uniform size distribution. The particle size is close to the calculated crystallite size from the XRD and lattice lines also confirms the good crystalline structure of rutile -type SnO2. Further, it can be seen from the HR-TEM images, N-CQDs were grafted on the SnO2 NPs to form the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite. The corresponding EDX spectrum (Fig. 2e) also depicts the presence of Sn, O, C, and N in the nanocomposite and evident for the formation of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS and CV studies on the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface properties of the modified electrodes can be analyzed using the EIS technique. Electrochemical charge transport performance on the bare SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, SnO2/SPCE, and N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE were studied by monitoring the charge transfer resistance (R ct) at the electrode and electrolyte interface in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- system. The impedance spectra were recorded in the frequency range of 10 mHz to 100 KHz, which are shown in Fig. 3 . The measured Rct value for the bare SPCE, N-CQD/SPCE, SnO2/SPCE, and N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE were 415, 101, 3300 and 1276 Ω cm−2, respectively. The Rct value of N-CQD/SnO2 modified SPCE is much lower than the SnO2 modified SPCE. The observed improvement in the charge transport nature of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE is well related with the proposal that the surface modification of SnO2 with N-CQD plays an essential role in the charge transport of the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite. This synergetic effect of N-CQD and the catalytic effect of SnO2 will be shown the high electrocatalytic response to the electroactive species.

The CVs of the SnO2 and N-CQD/SnO2 modified electrodes were recorded in the existence of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- in 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1 are displayed in Fig. 3B. Bare SPCE shows a pair of well-defined redox peak with a peak separation of 230 mV (ΔE p = (E pa − E pc)). But, for the N-CQD, SnO2, and N-CQD/SnO2 modified electrodes, the peak to peak separation values (ΔE p) are calculated to be 205, 215, and 207 mV respectively. The bare SPCE and SnO2/SPCE show the larger ΔE p values compare to that of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE. The larger peak separation is expected to consequence from the lower electrical conductivity. The obtained results are in good agreement with the R ct values resulted from EIS measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for electrooxidation of RF
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 2 shows the proposed mechanism tempted for the electrooxidation of RF mostly on reported research works [27–29]. The chemical structure of RF contains the benzene rings which are combined together by a γ-pyrene ring. The nitrogen atoms present in the N-CQD/SnO2composite might form hydrogen bonds with the hydrocarbyls present in the RF structure. Due to the formation of hydrogen bonding, it is easier to adsorb the RF on the electrode surface. This leads to the increase of RF concentration on the modified electrode surface. Further, the hydrogen bond between hydrocarbyl and nitrogen atom would decrease the OH bond energy and then activate the hydrocarbyl in the benzene ring [27]. In addition to that SnO2 also possess the good catalytic activity towards the oxidation process. Because of these factors, reaction kinetics is improved and also the electrochemical response of RF greatly enhanced at N-CQD/SnO2 modified electrode surface.

To examine the kinetics that occur in the electrochemical redox process, CV measurements were carried out over the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE in the presence of 25 μM RF at various scan rates (10–200 mV s−1) in PBS (pH 7.0). A linear increase in both anodic (I pa) and cathodic (I pc) peak currents with scan rate were observed, as shown in Fig. 4b. Furthermore, linear correlations were also obtained in the log-log plots of I pa and I pc vs. scan rate (Fig. 4d). And a slope value also closes to 1.0 (0.9997), suggesting an adsorption-controlled kinetics process on the surface of the modified SPCE [30]. On the basis of kinetic theory, when a diffusion-controlled process happens, a slope of 0.5 is expected [31]. Thus, it is confirming that the electrooxidation of RF on the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE is a surface-controlled adsorption process. The electrocatalytic reaction in RF takes place at flavin moiety of the structure, which is experiencing the two electrons and two protons transfer process during the redox reaction though, both oxidized and reduced forms make a quick equilibrium with semiquinone form, which is shown in Scheme 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of film thickness and pH
ce:section.ce:para: A film thickness of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite on the electrode surface was optimized. The effect of the film thickness of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite (1–10 mg mL−1) was examined using CV at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 with the presence of 25 μM RF, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 a. It can be seen from Fig. 5a, the higher current response results at 5 mg mL−1 thickness of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite. A further increment in the thickness of nanocomposite tends to the saturation level. Hence, 5 mg mL−1 thickness of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite is an optimized concentration for RF redox performance.

Further, the effect of different pH on the electrochemical response of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode for the detection of RF was carried out in the presence of 25 μM RF at 0.1 M PBS of different pH (3.0–11.0) range (Fig. 5b). It is observed that the redox potential varies with the change in pH; while the reversible reaction results at all the pH. However, the increment of pH from 3.0 to 7.0, anodic peak current response is increased steadily. Beyond the increase in pH 7.0 anodic peak current is decreased gradually (Fig. 5c). This may be due to the unstable nature of RF in the higher pH range. The maximum anodic peak current resulted at pH 7.0 for the electro-oxidation of RF at the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode. Even though, a less oxidation potential observed at pH 3.0 and pH 5.0 for the oxidation of RF, that pH levels are not suitable to determine the RF concentration in biological samples. Thus, PBS with pH 7.0 is an optimized pH to the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE electrode for the higher oxidation of RF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and detection limit of RF sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and detection limit of the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode for the detection of RF were calculated from DPV analysis in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). The DPVs of different concentration of RF (0.05–306 μM) at N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE were displayed in Fig. 6 a. Further, the increase in the concentration of RF leads to the increment of anodic peak current linearly. These results proved that the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite might afford better electrocatalytic activity towards RF sensing. Further, A linear plot was drawn between the anodic peak current and the concentration of RF (Fig. 6b). A linear regression equation may be stated as: 0.1952 [RF] (μM) + 12.639 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9950. From the slope of the linear plot, sensitivity is calculated, which is 2.496 μA μM−1 cm-2 and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) value is 8 nm. The obtained experimental results represent good sensitivity and lower detection limit of the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE for the sensitive detection of RF. Further, the analytical parameters of the proposed sensor are compared with other reported sensors, which is given in Table 1 .

The selectivity of the sensor was investigated by DPV measurements in the co-existence of some common interfering biomolecules such as uric acid (UA), L-ascorbic acid (AA), cystamine (Cy), dopamine (DA) and tyrosine (Ty). The DPV profiles of N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE are recorded with 50 μM of RF in the presence of 2000 μM concentration of UA, AA, DA, and Ty as shown in Fig. 7 a. The DPV current response is very slightly changed in the existence of a higher concentration of interfering biomolecules (Fig. 7b). The influence of interfering molecules is weak at short working potential (from -0.7 to -0.1 V). Because UA, AA, DA, and Ty possess the oxidation potential at positive region [37,38]. When expanding the working potential from -0.7 to 0.7 V the responses of AA, DA and UA are observed. However, the current response of RF is not changed at the higher concentration of interferants. Furthermore, the increase in the concentration of RF from 50 to 200 μM in the presence of interferants also gives the increasing peak current as shown in Fig. 7c. Thus, the findings clearly demonstrating that the common interfering biomolecules and metal ions made a negligible amount of change in the oxidation peak of RF. Thus, the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode is suitable for the sensitive detection of RF even in the presence of excess amount of interfering molecules and ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accumulation time, operational stability and reproducibility
ce:section.ce:para: An investigation of accumulation time of the analyte over the electrode surface can improve the determination and detection limit effectively. Hence the different accumulation time for the redox process of RF was carried out in the 25 μM RF in PBS (pH 7.0) at 50 mV s−1 scan rate. The accumulation time of 0–210 s with the interval of 30 s, CVs were recorded. The anodic peak current response of RF is steadily increased up to 90 s (Fig. 8 a). A further increase in accumulation time caused a sudden decrease and tend to the saturation level. Thus, 90 s is the optimum accumulation time for effective determination of the RF.

To ascertain the reproducibility five-different composite modified electrodes are prepared and their CV response for RF is observed. Their anodic peak current is similar to all the electrodes which are represented in Fig. 8b. Moreover, the operational stability of the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE was analyzed by running a continuous 50 continuous cycles of CVs in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 25 μM of RF at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Fig. 8c and d depict the CV response of the without and with the presence of analyte respectively at the composite modified electrode. In without presence of RF, the modified electrode (Fig. 8c) shows only 0.77% loss of current response from the initial value, but in the presence of RF current response is increased up to 10% due to the adsorption of RF over the surface through the hydrogen bonding. These results predict the good operational stability of the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE modified electrode. The above results show the good operational stability, reproducibility of the modified electrode and do not feel surface fouling. Thus, the fabricated RF sensor is highly stable and reproducible.

To assess the ability of the proposed RF sensor for practical usage, it was tested in real samples like milk powder, riboflavin tablet, and vitamin B complex tablet. The amount of RF present in pharmaceutical samples was determined, based on the DPV curves resulted in pharmaceutical products and the constructed calibration curve (Fig. 6b). By using the proposed sensor, the estimated amount of RF is in good agreement with the tagged amount RF content in the investigated tablets. In addition to that assess the performance of the proposed sensor, three different known concentration of standard RF are spiked into the tablet samples and then studied again. The obtained recovery results along with the RSD values are potted in Table 2 . Thus, the obtained results confirm that the successful applicability of the N-CQD/SnO2/SPCE sensing platform for the real sample analysis.

We have successfully prepared the N-CQD decorated SnO2 nanocomposite by simple ultra-sonication method for the first time. The surface modification was confirmed by X-ray diffraction, TEM and EIS studies. The N-CQD on the surface of SnO2 NPs not only enhance the conductivity but also prevent the agglomeration of SnO2 NPs. A DPV based sensing protocol for RF was developed using N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite which exhibits higher electrocatalytic activity than other modified electrodes. Moreover, the developed sensor also possesses excellent performance in stability, selectivity, sensitivity, and reproducibility. All these beneficial features can make the proposed sensor highly applicable in food and medical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank for the financial support of the UGC – BSR (25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7 – 88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship, India, MHRD-RUSA 2.0, New Delhi, India, and The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 106-2113-M-027-003). The authors also acknowledge Miss. Udhayabanu, M.A. English, for the language corrections.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Ganesan Muthusankar received his B. Sc. Degree (2013) from Alagappa Govt. Arts College, Tamilnadu, India. He received his M. Sc. degree (2015) from Alagappa University, Tamilnadu India. Now he is a third-year Ph.D., student in the Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Tamilnadu, India. His current research interest mainly focuses on the synthesis of hetero atom doped carbon/graphene quantum dots, novel metal oxides for electrochemical sensors. In addition, he is more interested in supercapacitor, photocatalyst and synthesis of inorganic metal complexes.

Mr. Chellakannu Rajkumar received his B.Sc. (2009) and M.Sc. (2012) degrees in Department of Chemistry, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. At present he is a third-year (Ph.D) student under the supervisor of Prof. Shen-Ming Chen in Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology at National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan. His research interest mainly focuses on the synthesis of nanomaterial for electrochemical sensors and biosensors. He also further devoted to nanomaterials for the catalytic applications.

Dr. Shen-Ming Chen received his B.S. Degree in Chemistry in 1980 from National Kaohsiung Normal University, Taiwan. He received his M.S. Degree (1983) and Ph.D. degree (1991) in Chemistry from National Taiwan University, Taiwan. He is currently a professor at the Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan. His current research interests include electroanalytical chemistry, bioelectrochemistry, fabrication of energy conservation and storage devices and nanomaterial synthesis for electrochemical applications. He has published more than 500 research articles in SCI journals.

Dr. G. Gopu received his M.Sc. degree (2002) from St. Joseph’s college, Tamilnadu, India. He received his Ph.D., (2009) from Alagappa University, Tamilnadu, India. He is currently Assistant Professor at Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Tamilnadu, India. His current research interests include electrochemical sensors, fabrication of energy storage devices and synthesis of nanomaterial for electrochemical sensor applications. He has published more than 10 research articles in SCI journals.

Mr. Arumugam Sangili received his B. Sc. Degree (2012) from M.S.S. Wakf Board College, Tamilnadu, India. He received his M. Sc. degree (2014) from Alagappa University, Tamilnadu India. Further, he also received master of Philosophy (2016) from Madurai Kamaraj University, Tamilnadu, India. Now he is a Ph.D., student in Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan. His current research interest mainly focuses on the synthesis of novel metal oxides and its composites for electrochemical sensors application.

Dr. N. Sengottuvelan received his M.Sc. degree (1997) and Ph.D., (2006) from University of Madras, Tamilnadu, He is currently Assistant Professor at Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Tamilnadu, India. His current research interests include synthesis of metal complexes and macrocyclic ligand contains metal complexes for DNA cleavage studies and OLEDs. He has published more than 50 research articles in SCI journals.
